text.title: Multi-Platform Design Parity Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Multi-Platform Design Parity application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Tokens (color, spacing, typography) shared as semantic names across platforms. Pixel values diverge per platform's design language (Material density vs HIG).
rule.rationale: Shared semantic names allow unified design system; pixel divergence respects platform conventions.

rule.statement: Maintain a matrix: gesture/pointer/voice/remote × platform. Component decisions specify which interactions are supported per platform.
rule.rationale: Components designed for one interaction model break in others; explicit matrix prevents shipping broken UX.

rule.statement: Three density tiers: compact (mobile/wearable), normal (web/tablet), comfortable (TV/large desktop). Tokens map to each tier.
rule.rationale: Density-blind designs feel cramped on small + sparse on large; explicit tiers solve both.

rule.statement: Parity ≠ identical. Each platform's native conventions take priority over visual identity. Material on Android, HIG on iOS, web idioms on web.
rule.rationale: Cross-platform visual identity at expense of native conventions = poor usability + 1-star reviews.

rule.statement: Document expected release cadence per platform (e.g., 'feature lands on web week 1, mobile week 2, TV next quarter'). Disclose to users.
rule.rationale: Silent staggered releases create 'feature on web only, why?' tickets; explicit cadence sets expectations.
